text:
Section 3(5) Joint liability for acts done by several persons in furtherance of common intention
When a criminal act is done by several persons in furtherance of the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone. This provision legally binds together all individuals participating in a group crime, ensuring they hold collective responsibility regardless of their direct physical contribution.

metadata.title: (5) Joint liability for acts done by several persons in furtherance of common intention

text:
Section 45 Abetment of a thing
A person abets the doing of a thing, who instigates any person to do that thing, or engages with one or more other persons in any conspiracy for the doing of that thing, or intentionally aids the doing of that thing. The legal responsibility extends past the primary offender directly to whoever orchestrates, aids, or advises the execution of the crime.

Section 64 Punishment for rape
Whoever commits rape shall be punished with rigorous imprisonment of either description for a term which shall not be less than ten years, but which may extend to imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine. This ensures uncompromising and severe deterrence against sexual violence.

Section 69 Sexual intercourse by employing deceitful means
Whoever, by deceitful means or making by promise to marry to a woman without any intention of fulfilling the same, and has sexual intercourse with her, such sexual intercourse not amounting to the offence of rape, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years and shall also be liable to fine.

metadata.title: Sexual intercourse by employing deceitful means

text:
Section 74 Assault or use of criminal force to woman with intent to outrage her modesty
Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any woman, intending to outrage or knowing it to be likely that he will thereby outrage her modesty, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine.

metadata.title: Assault or use of criminal force to woman with intent to outrage her modesty

text:
Section 75 Sexual harassment
A man committing acts like making physical contact and advances involving unwelcome and explicit sexual overtures, demanding sexual favours, showing pornography without consent, or making sexually coloured remarks, commits the offence of sexual harassment, and shall be punished decisively under the law.

Section 76 Voyeurism
Any man who watches, or captures the image of a woman engaging in a private act in circumstances where she would usually have the expectation of not being observed, disseminates such an image, shall be punished with imprisonment. First conviction dictates at least one and up to three years imprisonment.

Section 77 Stalking
Any man who follows a woman and repeatedly attempts to contact her to foster personal interaction despite clear indications of disinterest by the woman commits stalking. This also extensively covers the relentless monitoring of a woman's internet, email, or other electronic footprints.

Section 103 Punishment for murder
Whoever commits murder shall be punished with death or imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine. As the most extreme penalty under the Sanhita, this directly addresses cases demonstrating willful, premeditated malice leading to the extinguishment of human life.

Section 105 Culpable homicide not amounting to murder
Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any act with the intention of causing death, or with the intention of causing such bodily injury as is likely to cause death, without the presence of absolute malice aforethought found in a murder charge, is punishable under this section for culpable homicide.

metadata.title: Culpable homicide not amounting to murder

text:
Section 106 Causing death by negligence
Whoever causes the death of any person by doing any rash or negligent act not amounting to culpable homicide, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, and shall also be liable to fine. This is significantly deployed in catastrophic motor vehicle accidents or fatal industrial negligence.

Section 109 Attempt to murder
Whoever does any act with such intention or knowledge, and under such circumstances that, if he by that act caused death, he would be guilty of murder, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to ten years.

Section 111 Organised crime
Whoever, being a member of an organised crime syndicate or on behalf of such syndicate, commits organised crime, shall be punished intensely. The section explicitly recognizes the complex web of syndicated operations encompassing extortion, cyber-crimes, trafficking, or economic offences, dealing with them decisively rather than as isolated crimes.

Section 112 Terrorist act
Whoever does any act with the intent to threaten or likely to threaten the unity, integrity, security, economic security, or sovereignty of India, or with the intent to strike terror or likely to strike terror in the people, commits a terrorist act. The punishment for terrorist acts causing death includes the death penalty or imprisonment for life.

Section 113 Mob lynching and hate crimes
When a group of five or more persons acting in concert commits murder on the ground of race, caste or community, sex, place of birth, language, personal belief or any other similar ground, each member of such group shall be punished with death or with imprisonment for life, and shall also be liable to fine.

Section 115 Voluntarily causing hurt
Whoever does any act with the intention of thereby causing hurt to any person, or with the knowledge that he is likely thereby to cause hurt to any person, and does thereby cause hurt to any person, is said voluntarily to cause hurt, punishable with imprisonment ranging up to one year.

Section 117 Voluntarily causing grievous hurt
Whoever voluntarily causes grievous hurt, meaning inflicting severe and specific permanent bodily damages like deep fractures or irreversible organ impairment, faces heavy punishment. The law distinctly isolates this from standard physical altercations due to the life-altering impact on the victim.

Section 137 Kidnapping
Whoever kidnaps any person from India or forcibly extracts a minor from lawful guardianship is heavily prosecuted under this section. The focus lies squarely on the unauthorised disruption of legitimate custody or the violation of sovereign territorial borders to traffic humans.

Section 143 Trafficking of person
Whoever imports, exports, removes, buys, sells or disposes of any person as a slave, or accepts, receives or detains against his will any person as a slave, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to seven years, heavily restricting any form of institutional human trade.

Section 281 Rash driving
Whoever drives any vehicle, or rides, on any public way in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life, or to be likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to six months.

Section 303 Theft
Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, physically moves that property in order to achieve such taking, commits theft. Punishments vary substantially depending on whether the theft occurs in a dwelling house or out in the open.

Section 305 Snatching
Theft is snatching if, in order to commit theft, the offender suddenly or quickly or forcibly seizes or secures or grabs or takes away from any person or from his possession any movable property. This specifically targets the sudden and violent appropriation of chains, bags, or mobile phones directly from pedestrians.

Section 308 Extortion
Whoever intentionally puts any person in fear of any injury to that person, or to any other, and thereby dishonestly induces the person so put in fear to deliver to any person any property or valuable security, commits extortion. The core difference against theft is the reliance on explicit threats to gain the property.

Section 309 Robbery
Robbery acts as an aggravated form of theft or extortion, occurring when the perpetrator actively utilizes or threatens instant physical violence, wrongful restraint, or even death, in order to successfully execute the theft or retain the wrongfully acquired property.

Section 310 Dacoity
When five or more persons conjointly commit or attempt to commit a robbery, or where the whole number of persons conjointly committing or attempting to commit a robbery, and persons present and aiding such commission or attempt, amount to five or more, every person so committing, attempting or aiding, is said to commit dacoity.

Section 314 Criminal breach of trust
Whoever, being in any manner entrusted with property, or with any dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use that property, commits criminal breach of trust. This addresses white-collar betrayals like an employee diverting corporate funds to their private accounts.

Section 316 Criminal misappropriation of property
Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts to his own use any movable property, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine, or with both. This applies often to cases where someone finds lost property and illegally keeps it despite knowing the true owner.

Section 318 Cheating
Whoever, by deceiving any person, fraudulently or dishonestly induces the person so deceived to deliver any property to any person, or to consent that any person shall retain any property, commits cheating. This broad definition encompasses everything from sophisticated investment frauds to simple online marketplace deceptions.

Section 336 Forgery
Whoever makes any false documents or false electronic record or part of a document or electronic record, with intent to cause damage or injury, to the public or to any person, or to support any claim or title, or to cause any person to part with property, commits forgery.

Section 351 Criminal intimidation
Whoever threatens another with any injury to his person, reputation or property, or to the person or reputation of any one in whom that person is interested, with intent to cause alarm to that person, commits criminal intimidation. The offence inherently destabilizes the victim's psychological security and compels them into acting against their free will.

Section 352 Intentional insult with intent to provoke breach of peace
Whoever intentionally insults, and thereby gives provocation to any person, intending or knowing it to be likely that such provocation will cause him to break the public peace, or to commit any other offence, is prosecutable. The verbal or physical insult must carry a deliberate objective to incite an aggressive public reaction.

metadata.title: Intentional insult with intent to provoke breach of peace

text:
Section 354 Public nuisance
A person is guilty of a public nuisance who does any act or is guilty of an illegal omission which causes any common injury, danger or annoyance to the public or to the people in general who dwell or occupy property in the vicinity, or which must necessarily cause injury, obstruction, danger or annoyance to persons who may have occasion to use any public right.

Section 356 Defamation
Whoever, by words either spoken or intended to be read, or by signs or by visible representations, makes or publishes any imputation concerning any person intending to harm, or knowing or having reason to believe that such imputation will harm, the reputation of such person, commits defamation.

CONSUMER PROTECTION ACT
Right to safety
Consumers must be protected against goods and services that are hazardous to life and property.
Right to information
Consumers have the right to know product quality, quantity, price, ingredients, and manufacturer details before purchase.
Right to choose
Consumers have access to a variety of goods and services at competitive prices without unfair restrictions.
Right to redressal
Consumers can seek compensation for defective goods, poor service, or unfair trade practices.
Right to be heard
Consumer interests must be considered at appropriate forums when disputes arise.
Defective goods
Any fault, imperfection, or shortcoming in quality, quantity, or performance makes a product defective and eligible for replacement or refund.
Deficiency in service
Any inadequacy or poor quality in service (delivery, repair, etc.) is considered deficiency and is punishable.
Unfair trade practice

y or poor quality in service (delivery, repair, etc.) is considered deficiency and is punishable.
Unfair trade practice
False advertising, misleading claims, hidden charges, or deceptive pricing are punishable.
E-commerce consumer protection
Online sellers must provide accurate product details, transparent pricing, return policies, and refund mechanisms.
Delayed delivery
Failure to deliver goods or services within promised time is a deficiency in service.
Refund and cancellation issues
Refusal or delay in refund after product return or cancellation is actionable under consumer law.
Overcharging
Charging more than the printed price (MRP) or agreed price is illegal.
Fake or counterfeit products
Selling fake or duplicate products is punishable under consumer protection laws.
Warranty and guarantee violation
Failure to honor warranty or guarantee commitments is considered deficiency in service.
Product liability

tee violation
Failure to honor warranty or guarantee commitments is considered deficiency in service.
Product liability
Manufacturers, sellers, and service providers are responsible for harm caused by defective products.
Misleading advertisements
Advertisements that give false promises or exaggerate product features are punishable.
Hidden charges
Charging undisclosed fees or additional costs without informing the consumer is illegal.
Online fraud in purchases
If an online seller takes payment but does not deliver goods, it is considered unfair trade practice and fraud.
Poor after-sales service
Failure to provide promised support or service after purchase is a deficiency in service.
Damage during delivery
If goods are damaged during delivery, the seller or platform is responsible for compensation or replacement.
Consumer complaint filing
Consumers can file complaints in consumer forums or online portals for dispute resolution.
Jurisdiction of consumer courts

sumers can file complaints in consumer forums or online portals for dispute resolution.
Jurisdiction of consumer courts
Consumers can file complaints in district, state, or national consumer commissions based on claim value.
Unfair contract terms
Contracts that heavily favor sellers and harm consumer rights can be challenged.
Return and replacement rights
Consumers are entitled to return or replace defective or incorrect products within policy terms.
Digital services complaints
Issues with online subscriptions, apps, or digital services fall under consumer protection law.

Section 420 Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property
Whoever cheats and dishonestly induces a person to deliver property shall be punished with imprisonment up to seven years and fine.

metadata.title: Cheating and dishonestly inducing delivery of property

text:
Section 406 Criminal breach of trust
Dishonest misappropriation of entrusted property is punishable with imprisonment or fine.

Section 409 Criminal breach of trust by public servant
Public servants committing breach of trust face severe punishment including life imprisonment.

metadata.title: Criminal breach of trust by public servant

text:
Section 378 Theft
Taking movable property without consent with dishonest intention is theft.

Section 379 Punishment for theft
Punishable with imprisonment up to three years or fine.

Section 380 Theft in dwelling house
Theft inside a house or building is punishable with higher penalties.

Section 392 Robbery
Theft accompanied by violence or threat is robbery.

Section 394 Voluntarily causing hurt in robbery
Causing injury during robbery increases punishment.

Section 415 Cheating
Deceiving a person to deliver property or consent is cheating.

Section 463 Forgery
Creating false documents with intent to cause harm is forgery.

Section 465 Punishment for forgery
Forgery is punishable with imprisonment or fine.

Section 468 Forgery for purpose of cheating
Forgery intended for cheating is punishable with imprisonment up to seven years.

Section 471 Using forged document
Using forged documents as genuine is punishable.

Section 499 Defamation
Harming someone's reputation through statements is defamation.

Section 500 Punishment for defamation
Punishable with imprisonment up to two years or fine.

Section 503 Criminal intimidation
Threatening a person with injury is criminal intimidation.

Section 506 Punishment for criminal intimidation
Punishment depends on severity of threat.

Section 299 Culpable homicide
Causing death with intention or knowledge constitutes culpable homicide.

Section 300 Murder
Culpable homicide becomes murder under specific conditions.

Section 302 Punishment for murder
Punishable with death or life imprisonment.

Section 304 Culpable homicide not amounting to murder
Punishment varies based on intention and knowledge.

metadata.title: Culpable homicide not amounting to murder

text:
Section 323 Voluntarily causing hurt
Causing physical pain or injury is punishable.

Section 325 Grievous hurt
Serious injuries attract stricter punishment.

Section 341 Wrongful restraint
Restricting someone’s movement unlawfully is punishable.

Section 351 Assault
Threatening or attempting to cause harm is assault.

Section 354 Assault on woman
Outraging modesty of a woman is punishable.

Section 354A Sexual harassment
Unwelcome advances or physical contact constitute sexual harassment.

Section 354D Stalking
Repeatedly following or contacting a woman is punishable.

Section 376 Rape
Punishable with rigorous imprisonment not less than ten years.

Section 509 Insulting modesty of woman
Words or gestures insulting modesty are punishable.

Section 34 Common intention
All persons involved in a crime share liability.

Section 43 Penalty and compensation for damage to computer system
If any person without permission of the owner or any other person who is in charge of a computer, computer system or computer network accesses or secures access to such computer network or downloads any data, they are completely liable to pay damages by way of compensation to the person so affected.

metadata.title: Penalty and compensation for damage to computer system

text:
Section 43A Compensation for failure to protect data
Where a body corporate, possessing, dealing or handling any sensitive personal data or information in a computer resource which it owns, controls or operates, is negligent in implementing and maintaining reasonable security practices, leading to wrongful loss or wrongful gain to any person, such body corporate shall be liable to pay damages by way of compensation.

metadata.title: Compensation for failure to protect data

text:
Section 65 Tampering with computer source documents
Whoever knowingly or intentionally conceals, destroys or alters or intentionally or knowingly causes another to conceal, destroy, or alter any computer source code used for a computer, computer programme, computer system or computer network, when the computer source code is required to be kept or maintained by law, shall be punished with imprisonment up to three years.

Section 66 Computer related offences
If any person, dishonestly or fraudulently, does any act referred to in section 43, he shall be punishable with imprisonment for a term which may extend to three years or with fine which may extend to five lakh rupees or with both. This acts as the backbone for prosecuting general hacking and unauthorized manipulation.

Section 66B Punishment for dishonestly receiving stolen computer resource
Whoever dishonestly receives or retains any stolen computer resource or communication device knowing or having reason to believe the same to be stolen computer resource or communication device, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years or with fine.

metadata.title: Punishment for dishonestly receiving stolen computer resource

text:
Section 66C Punishment for identity theft
Whoever, fraudulently or dishonestly make use of the electronic signature, password or any other unique identification feature of any other person, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine. This is the primary legal tool against impersonation and unauthorized account takeovers.

Section 66D Punishment for cheating by personation by using computer resource
Whoever, by means for any communication device or computer resource cheats by personating, shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to three years and shall also be liable to fine. This addresses sophisticated phishing attacks and social engineering ploys executed digitally.

metadata.title: Punishment for cheating by personation by using computer resource

text:
Section 66E Punishment for violation of privacy
Whoever, intentionally or knowingly captures, publishes or transmits the image of a private area of any person without his or her consent, under circumstances violating the privacy of that person, shall be punished with imprisonment which may extend to three years or with fine not exceeding two lakh rupees.

Section 66F Punishment for cyber terrorism
Whoever, with intent to threaten the unity, integrity, security or sovereignty of India or to strike terror in the people, denies access to authorised personnel to a computer resource, or attempts to penetrate or access a computer resource without authorisation, commits cyber terrorism, carrying a potential punishment of imprisonment for life.

Section 67 Punishment for publishing obscene material in electronic form
Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted in the electronic form, any material which is lascivious or appeals to the prurient interest, shall be punished. On first conviction, this can lead to imprisonment of either description for a term extending to three years and significant fines.

metadata.title: Punishment for publishing obscene material in electronic form

text:
Section 67A Punishment for publishing sexually explicit act in electronic form
Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted in the electronic form any material which contains sexually explicit act or conduct shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to five years, highlighting a stark escalation in punishment from standard obscene materials.

metadata.title: Punishment for publishing sexually explicit act in electronic form

text:
Section 67B Punishment for publishing material depicting children in sexually explicit act
Whoever publishes or transmits or causes to be published or transmitted material in any electronic form which depicts children engaged in sexually explicit act or conduct; or creates text or digital images, gathers, seeks, or browses such material, faces exceedingly severe punishments to fiercely protect the digital safety of minors.

metadata.title: Punishment for publishing material depicting children in sexually explicit act

text:
Section 67C Preservation and retention of information by intermediaries
Intermediary shall preserve and retain such information as may be specified for such duration and in such manner and format as the Central Government may prescribe. This mandate legally requires telecom providers and internet platforms to securely hold onto user digital trails to assist ongoing cybercrime investigation efforts.

metadata.title: Preservation and retention of information by intermediaries

text:
Section 69 Power to issue directions for interception or decrypting information
Where the Central Government or a State Government is satisfied that it is necessary in the interest of the sovereignty or integrity of India, defense of India, or security of the State, it may securely direct any agency to intercept, monitor or decrypt any information generated, transmitted, received or stored in any computer resource.

metadata.title: Power to issue directions for interception or decrypting information

text:
Section 69A Power to issue directions for blocking for public access of any information
Where it is necessary in the interest of sovereignty and integrity of India, defence of India, or security of the State, the Government can directly instruct intermediaries or authorities to block citizen access to specific dangerous web resources, actively preventing the spread of digital sabotage or systemic panic.

metadata.title: Power to issue directions for blocking for public access of any information

text:
Section 71 Penalty for misrepresentation
Whoever makes any misrepresentation to, or suppresses any material fact from, the Controller or the Certifying Authority for obtaining any licence or electronic signature Certificate, shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to one lakh rupees, or with both.

Section 72 Penalty for breach of confidentiality and privacy
Save as otherwise provided in this Act or any other law for the time being in force, if any person who has secured access to any electronic record, book, register, correspondence, information, document or other material without the consent of the person concerned discloses such material to any other person, they face criminal prosecution.

metadata.title: Penalty for breach of confidentiality and privacy

text:
Section 72A Punishment for disclosure of information in breach of lawful contract
Any person including an intermediary who, while providing services under the terms of lawful contract, has secured access to any material containing personal information about another person, with the intent to cause wrongful loss or wrongful gain, directly discloses that material unconditionally, commits a punishable offence.

metadata.title: Punishment for disclosure of information in breach of lawful contract

text:
Section 73 Penalty for publishing electronic signature Certificate false in certain particulars
No person shall publish a electronic signature Certificate or otherwise make it available to any other person with the knowledge that the Certifying Authority listed in such certificate has not issued it, or the subscriber listed hasn't accepted it. Violations aim to severely restrict the unchecked proliferation of fraudulent digital security credentials.

metadata.title: Penalty for publishing electronic signature Certificate false in certain particulars

text:
Section 74 Publication for fraudulent purpose
Whoever knowingly creates, publishes or otherwise makes available an Electronic Signature Certificate for any fraudulent or unlawful purpose shall be punished with imprisonment for a term which may extend to two years, or with fine which may extend to one lakh rupees, or with both.

Section 75 Act to apply for offence or contravention committed outside India
Subject to the provisions of this Act, the provisions of this Act shall apply also to any offence or contravention committed outside India by any person irrespective of his nationality, strictly provided that the act involves a computer, computer system or computer network located deeply within sovereign Indian territory.

metadata.title: Act to apply for offence or contravention committed outside India
